turns.text: Hello Clara, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for... for wanting to listen. It is hard to find people here who have the time. 

My mother, she used to say that pregnancy is a season of waiting, but here, it feels more like a race. Every time I go to the clinic in Beverly, everything is so fast. The nurses, they smile, but they talk in a way that makes my head spin. They use words like "gestational" and "prenatal screenings," and I just nod, even when I am drowning in the sounds of it. I am twenty-one, and I feel very small in those big chairs. 

Back home, I helped so many women with their babies. I knew how to help them start the nursing, how to hold them so they feel safe. But here, I feel like I have forgotten everything. I worry that because I am alone—no family here, just me—the doctors look at me and see someone who doesn't know anything. When they ask about my "birth plan," I want to cry. How can I plan the birth when I am still trying to figure out how to navigate the bus schedule to get to my appointments? I just want to be a good mother, but I am so tired of feeling like a stranger in my own body. Is it always this lonely to grow a life?

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

I imagined the clinic would be a place of... *tranquilidad*. Where I come from, the midwife is like an auntie. You go, she holds your hand, she touches your belly, and she asks about your dreams for the baby. We talk about the *susto*—the fear—and she gives you teas and advice that makes you feel strong. I thought I would walk into the office here and find someone who would see me, not just my file.

But the reality... it is just bright lights and clicking keyboards. Everything is very white and very loud. They put these cold sensors on me, and they look at the screen more than they look at my face. I expected someone to ask me how my heart is doing, but they only ask about my iron, my weight, my "risk factors." 

I feel like a *número*—a number on a paper. I want to tell them, "I know about the gold milk, the colostrum, I know the baby needs me to be calm," but the words, they get stuck in my throat. I am afraid if I speak slow, they will think I am not smart. So I just stay quiet. I thought I would feel empowered, but instead, I leave feeling like I am doing it all wrong.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest surprise, I think, was how much they focus on what I *cannot* do, rather than what I already know. 

I came into the office with so much pride in my heart. I have held newborns, I have cleaned umbilical cords, and I have seen the way a mother’s gaze can quiet a crying child. I expected that we would share that knowledge, like a bridge between my world and theirs. Instead, they handed me these thick packets of paper—so many papers—filled with warnings. *Don’t eat this. Don’t do that. Watch for this danger.* 

It was a shock, honestly. It made me feel like I was a child who needed to be scolded, not a woman becoming a mother. The negative part is that it made me doubt my own hands. I look at my fingers now and wonder, "Will I be enough?" I thought they would ask me what I needed to feel safe, but they only told me what the clinic requires. 

But... maybe there is one small, good thing. The ultrasound tech, she didn't speak much Spanish, but she saw me tremble when the screen turned on. She reached out and touched my shoulder for just a second. That touch? It felt like home for a moment. It was the only time I felt seen.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I expected a conversation. You know, like two people sitting in a kitchen, drinking coffee, where the doctor would look me in the eye and say, "Clara, the baby is strong, she is growing just as she should." I wanted to hear that *mi hija* is perfect. I expected to ask questions—about the food I should eat, about how to keep my mind quiet—and to get answers that felt like advice from a friend.

The reality is so... detached. The results come in a way that feels like a cold wind. It is a notification on my phone, or a letter in the mail, or the midwife reading a list of numbers while she is already halfway out the door. When they do speak to me, it is all "levels" and "percentiles." It sounds like math, not a baby. 

I don't know how to ask, "Why are my levels like this?" without feeling like I am challenging them. I worry if I ask too much, I will seem like a *molestia*—a nuisance. So I just look at the paper and try to translate the words in my head, searching for the word "healthy." It is just me and the paper, alone in my apartment, trying to understand if my baby is safe. It shouldn't be this lonely to learn if your child is doing well.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

Oh, the gap is so wide, it feels like I am standing on one side of a canyon and the truth is on the other. They give me so much paper—graphs, charts, lists of symptoms—but none of it tells me *how* I am supposed to feel. They tell me my baby is "in the range," but they never tell me if she is happy in there. I want to know if she hears my voice when I sing those songs from back home. Does the stress of my work, the worry about the rent... does she feel that in her heart?

The information they give is for my body, like it is a machine that needs oil. But I am not a machine. I am a mother who is far from her own mother. I need someone to say, "Clara, you are doing a good job, and your baby is strong because you are strong." 

Instead, I get these cold facts, and I have to go to the internet to try to understand what "normal" means. It makes me feel like I am failing, because if I had my mother here, I wouldn’t need these papers. I would just know. This "care" feels like it misses the most important part: my spirit. I am starving for reassurance, and they are just feeding me data.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I really thought—I truly hoped—that I would have one person. Just one. Someone I could look at and see a familiar face, someone who would remember my name without having to look at the screen first. I wanted someone who knew about the time before this, someone who understood why my hands shake when I’m on the exam table, because they would know about the loss I carried before this baby came. 

But the reality? It is a revolving door. Every time I go back to that office in Beverly, it is a new face. A new nurse, a new midwife, or sometimes a doctor I have never seen before. I have to tell my story over and over, like a broken record. "Yes, I am twenty-one. Yes, this is my first pregnancy. Yes, I am working." 

It feels like every time I start to build a little bit of trust, they rotate me to someone else. I feel like a student who has to introduce herself to a new teacher every single day. How can they possibly know me? How can they know *my* baby if they don't know *me*? I am just a visitor in their busy day, and it makes me want to close up. It is very hard to be vulnerable when you are constantly starting from the beginning.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

It drains me, honestly. It is like carrying a heavy suitcase up a hill, and every time I get close to the top, someone tells me I have to start at the bottom again. 

Emotionally, it creates a wall. I stop trying to explain how I feel, because why should I tell my heart’s secrets to someone who will be gone by my next appointment? I start to give only "yes" or "no" answers. It’s safer that way. When I see a new face, I have to be "Clara the Patient" instead of just "Clara." I have to perform being calm, even if I am terrified. 

Practically, it is a mess. I spend so much time repeating the same things—my history, my concerns, the way I am feeling—that we run out of time for the real questions. I wanted to talk about how to prepare my body for breastfeeding, but we spend fifteen minutes just getting them caught up on who I am. I leave the office feeling like I’ve left behind a trail of little pieces of myself, and I have to go back and pick them up next time. It makes me feel invisible. How can they protect my health if they don’t actually know the person they are treating? They treat the pregnancy, but they are missing the mother.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I expected to be treated like a woman—a woman who is doing something miraculous. I thought when they examined my belly, it would be done with a little bit of reverence, maybe a soft touch, acknowledging that this is a home for a new soul. Back home, even when we are poor, there is a respect for the *madre*. The midwife would talk to the baby, sometimes even hum a little while she felt the position.

But here, it feels like I am at the mechanic. They are very clinical. The midwife’s hands are always cold, and she moves quickly, measuring the size, checking the heart rate, and then—*click*—she’s done. There is no warmth, no "Oh, she’s moving today, look at that." It feels very mechanical, like I am just a shell that holds the baby. 

Sometimes, I feel embarrassed, like my body is something to be poked and prodded without asking if I am ready. It makes me want to cover myself up and hide. I wanted them to talk to me about the changes in my body—to tell me it’s okay that I’m tired, or to show me how to care for my skin—but instead, they only talk about my weight. That hurts, you know? They look at the scale and they sigh, but they don't look at my eyes to see if I’m eating enough to keep us both alive. It feels like they are measuring a product, not a mother and child.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

There was one time, a few weeks ago. A new doctor—a man I hadn’t seen before—came into the room. He didn’t really introduce himself, just started talking about my BMI. He started saying words like "weight gain" and "management" and he used a tone that was very firm, like he was scolding a student who didn't do her homework. 

I felt my face get hot, that deep, prickling heat that happens when you are ashamed. I am trying so hard, you know? I work, I walk to the market, I eat the greens—I spend my last dollars on fresh things because I want my baby to be strong. And he made me feel like I was just failing, like I was being lazy or careless. 

He didn't ask what I was eating. He didn't ask about the stress of being alone or how much I am on my feet. He just looked at the chart and made a comment about "excessive" growth. I felt so small, like I was shrinking in that gown. I wanted to tell him, "Please, I am trying to be everything for her," but my English just... it failed me. I couldn't find the words to defend myself. I just stayed silent, staring at the floor, and felt like a bad mother already. It wasn't an examination of my body; it felt like an indictment of my character.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

Thankfully, the doctors tell me my blood is okay for now—they call it "low-risk." But even hearing that word, *risk*, makes my stomach turn. I think about my mother’s sister, who had the *pre-eclampsia*, and how sick she got. Before I was pregnant, I thought that if something like that happened, the doctors would be your partners. I imagined them sitting with you, explaining the *why* behind the food, maybe even helping you find ways to get the right things to eat if you didn't have much money.

The reality, from what I see in the waiting room and hear from other women, is that it is just more fear. If you have those conditions, the doctors don’t explain them like a teacher; they explain them like a judge. They give you a list of "Don’t" and they make it sound like if you eat the wrong thing, you are hurting your baby on purpose. 

It scares me to think about having a complication. If I am already having trouble understanding them, and if I am already feeling alone, what would happen if I were really sick? I don't think I would get comfort. I think I would just get more noise, more anxiety, and more papers to sign. It makes me want to pray that I stay "low-risk" forever, just so I don't have to face that mountain alone.
What was missing from the support offered — information, emotional acknowledgement, practical help?

Everything is missing, in a way. 

The biggest thing is *humanity*. They give me information, yes, but it is just information for my brain, not my heart. They tell me my baby’s heart rate is 140, but they never tell me, "Clara, you are doing a good job." I need someone to say that. I need someone to acknowledge that being twenty-one, far from my country, and alone in this city is a very heavy thing to carry while building a life inside me. 

Practically, I feel so isolated. I don't have a mother here to show me how to prepare for the *parto*—the labor—or how to set up the house. I thought the medical team might be that bridge. Maybe a class, or just a conversation about what it feels like to be scared of the pain, or how to handle the first few nights when the baby comes. Instead, I am just given brochures with pictures of white babies and perfect nurseries that look nothing like my life.

I am starving for someone to just ask, "How are you coping?" and really wait for the answer. I feel like I am walking a tightrope, and the people who are supposed to be holding the safety net are busy looking at their clipboards instead of watching me. I don't need another pamphlet. I need a person.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected that if I was worried, I could pick up the phone and speak to someone—someone who would know my name and understand that I am not calling just to hear my own voice. I thought that because I am carrying a life, there would be a door that is always open.

But the reality is a maze of telephone menus. *Press one for this, press two for that.* I press the buttons, and then I wait on hold for so long that I start to lose my courage. When I finally talk to someone, they are usually a receptionist, not a nurse. I have to explain my worry to them, but they just tell me to wait for the next appointment or that they will "leave a message" for the midwife. 

I once felt a pain—it was probably just the baby stretching, but I didn't know—and I called because I was so frightened I couldn't breathe. They made me feel like I was a problem. They told me to go to the Emergency Room if I was "really worried," but that place is even more terrifying, with the noise and the long hours of waiting in the cold. It feels like the system is designed to keep you away, not to help you. I want to be a strong mother, but how can I be strong when the place that is supposed to help me is so hard to reach?
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

In my mind, before the baby was even a whisper, I imagined a house full of women. I thought of my mother’s kitchen, the smell of cilantro and onion, the soft hands of my *tías* braiding my hair while we talked about the baby’s name. I expected a community that would hold me up so I wouldn't have to carry everything alone—someone to help me understand the American doctors, someone to tell me, "Clara, this is normal," so I wouldn't have to be afraid every time the baby moved.

The reality here in Beverly is… very quiet. My friends are also working, trying to survive, and they are tired too. It is not that they don't care, but they are fighting their own battles in this country. And my family, they are thousands of miles away. Sometimes, I call my mother, and I try to describe the clinic, the cold lights, the way the doctor looks at the screen instead of me, and she tries to comfort me. But she is so far away that her voice feels thin, like it’s coming from behind a glass wall.

There is no "village" here for me. I am building this nest with only my two hands and a lot of loneliness. I go to the park sometimes and see other mothers, but we are all in our own bubbles. I wish I had that community, someone to show me that I am enough, but instead, I have to be the one to remind myself every night when I am alone in my room.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I think I expected that someone—an auntie, a cousin, or even just another woman from my town who had already gone through this here—would come by and just *know* what I needed. I expected someone to show up with a pot of soup or a piece of cloth for the baby and say, "Clara, let me help you." That hasn't really happened. Everyone is so busy trying to keep their own heads above water, working long hours, that asking for help feels like an unfair burden. 

But, there *was* a surprise. My neighbor, she is not from my country—she speaks a different language, maybe Russian?—but she saw me struggling to carry my groceries up the stairs the other day. She didn't say much, just took the heavy bags from my hands and carried them right to my door. She didn’t know about the baby or the doctor’s office or the loneliness, but she saw that I was a girl trying to carry too much. 

That was a support I didn’t expect, and it felt very precious. It made me realize that even if I don’t have my family here, there might be other women who understand the weight of being a stranger. I still miss the village I grew up with, the ones who would have sat with me through the long nights. I realize now that the support I expected—the kind that comes from blood—isn't here, so I have to be open to these small, quiet kindnesses from strangers. It’s not the same, but it helps me breathe a little easier.

It is a strange thing, being a mother when you still feel like a child yourself. Every night, I lie in bed and feel her kick—this little heartbeat that I am responsible for—and I realize that I am the only one who truly knows her yet. I worry if she can sense my fear, if she can feel the way I hold my breath when I’m at the clinic. I wonder if she knows that her mother is doing this all for her, that she is the reason I wake up and try to navigate this city in a language that still feels like it’s fighting me.

I keep thinking about the "birth plan" they asked for. I want to tell them that my plan isn't about medicine or rooms or time. My plan is to make sure she feels loved the second she enters this world. I want to be the one who whispers to her in Spanish, so she knows her roots even if we are a long way from home. I want to be the one who holds her and doesn't let anyone take her away too quickly. 

I just hope that when the time comes, I don't let the white lights and the fast-talking nurses make me forget who I am. I hope I can be the mother I know I can be, even if the system here is built for someone else. I have to believe that my love is a language she will understand, even if I struggle to find the right words for the doctors. I am just hoping, really, that she will see me—the real me—and know she is safe.
Were there moments when a doctor, partner, or family member's opinion overrode yours — even when you hadn't asked for their input?

Yes, this happens more often than I can count. It is like everyone—the doctors, the nurses, even strangers on the bus who see my belly—suddenly thinks they are the experts on *my* life.

At the clinic, it is the worst. I remember once, I asked if I could try a different way to sit because my back was hurting, and the nurse just shook her head, almost laughing, and told me, "No, this is how we do it here, it’s safer." She didn't even stop to see if I was in pain; she just cared about how she was trained. I felt like a child, like my own body’s messages were not as important as her protocol.

Then there are the people in my life—even back home on the phone—who tell me, "You should do this, you should eat that, you should be this way." They mean well, but it feels like the baby is not even mine yet. It feels like everyone is grabbing at my life, trying to pull me in ten different directions. 

I just want to scream, "It is my baby!" I am the one feeling her move, I am the one who has to carry her through the labor, I am the one who will be up at 3:00 AM. Why is my voice the quietest one in the room? Every time they tell me what to do without asking what I feel, it makes me feel like I am losing my grip on my own motherhood. I don't want to be a patient; I want to be a mother. Why is that so hard to explain?

I look at my hands sometimes—the same hands that once soothed babies back home—and I wonder how they will hold my own child in this place. I am terrified that when I go into that hospital, the machines and the masks will make me feel even more like an outsider. 

I’ve started to dream about her. In my dreams, we are back by the river, and there are no white walls or forms to sign. It’s just us. I’m not scared of the weight of the baby; I’m scared of the weight of the *expectations*. I want to tell the doctors, "I am not just a pregnancy." I am someone who remembers how to sing, someone who knows how to pray, someone who has walked across a border just to give her a chance at a life that doesn't feel like a struggle. 

If I could say one thing to them, it would be this: *Please, just look at me.* Don't look at the screen, don't look at the chart, don't look at my age or my status. Just look at the mother. If you saw me, truly saw me, you would see that I already have everything she needs. I don't need you to tell me how to be a mother; I just need you to help me stay safe while I become one. I hope that when she finally arrives, the silence in the room will be broken by her cry, and for the first time in this whole journey, I will feel like I finally have a voice. I just hope I am brave enough to use it.
Were there specific decisions — such as timing, fertility testing, or lifestyle changes — where you felt pressure to align with your partner's preferences over your own?

It is... a hard thing to say aloud, even to you. You see, being an unmarried woman here, it already makes me feel as if I am walking on thin glass. Back home, things were clearer, but here, the path I walk with him—the father—is filled with shadows I didn't expect.

He wants everything to be "by the book," as he says. He worries so much about the money, the papers, and what the people back home will say if they find out I am doing this alone, that he pushes me to follow the doctors' instructions without question. When I tell him, "But I don't feel right about this," or "I want to try something else," he just looks at me and says, "Clara, these are the experts. Don't cause trouble. Do what they say." 

He thinks that by being "compliant," as they call it, we will be safe. But I feel like I am losing my own voice in his fear. Every time I suggest a different way—maybe a different way to prepare for the birth, or a way to keep my own spirit calm—he makes me feel like I am being difficult. It’s like he and the doctors are on one side, and I am on the other, standing alone. I don't want to fight him, but I also don't want to disappear. It is very hard to be a mother when you feel you have to ask permission to trust your own instincts.

I think about the day she arrives, and I wonder if that will be the moment everything changes. Maybe the doctors will see me then, holding her, and they will realize that I am not just a number or a patient in a gown. Maybe they will see the look in my eyes and understand that I have been carrying her, and this struggle, for a long time. 

But sometimes, I catch my reflection in the clinic window—the tired eyes, the loose clothes—and I feel like I am just fading away. I see the nurses, and they are so efficient, so *clinical*, and I realize they have seen thousands of women like me. It makes me wonder if I am just another story they tell each other in the breakroom when the day is done. 

I just want to be remembered as the woman who fought to be a good mother, not the woman who had trouble with her English or her iron levels. I want to tell my daughter, when she is old enough, that I was brave. I want her to know that even when I was alone, even when the lights were too bright and the words were too hard, I was always, *always* listening to her. I was the one who kept her safe in my heart when the world felt too big and too cold. 

If I can just make it to that day, I think I will finally feel like I belong somewhere. Not in the clinic, not in this city, but in her arms. That is the only place I am waiting for.
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

It happens all the time. It is like once you are pregnant, your body becomes public property—something everyone feels they have the right to comment on. 

At the clinic, it is the scale that starts the conversation. The nurse will look at the number, click her tongue, and say, "You’re gaining too quickly, Clara. You need to watch your portions." She says it like she’s telling me the weather, but it feels like a heavy stone dropping into my chest. She doesn't know that on the days I am too tired to cook, or when I have no money left for fresh vegetables, I eat whatever I can find to just stop the hunger. When she speaks like that, I don't feel supported; I feel judged. I feel like she thinks I am being careless with the life growing inside me.

Even away from the clinic, people have opinions. Sometimes women in the neighborhood or even acquaintances will look at my bump and say, "Oh, you’re getting so big!" or "Are you sure you aren't eating too much bread?" They don’t see that every extra pound is me trying to build a safe home for her. 

Those comments... they make me shrink. I start to cover my belly with my arms, trying to hide. I feel like I am failing a test I didn't even know I was taking. It makes me feel that my body is a site of failure rather than a miracle. Instead of feeling proud of the life I am carrying, I end up feeling ashamed of the space it takes up. Why is it so hard for them to see a mother instead of just a weight on a scale?

Sometimes, the most hurtful thing is not what they say, but what they leave unsaid. They focus on the scale and the blood tests, but they never ask about the nights when the silence in my apartment feels like a physical weight on my shoulders. They don't ask how I manage when the loneliness is so sharp I can’t eat. They just see the *numbers*.

If I could stand up in that room, without my heart racing or my voice shaking, I would tell them that I am not just a body that is growing a baby. I am a woman who has traveled across the world, a woman who has worked until her feet bled, a woman who is learning how to be a mother in a place that feels like it’s waiting for me to trip. I want them to know that every time they speak to me with that tone—that "expert" tone—they are taking a little more of my confidence away. 

I don't need them to be my family. I know they can't be that. But I need them to be my partners, not my judges. I need them to see that when I look down at my belly, I am not seeing "gestational progress." I am seeing my daughter, my life, my only reason for being here. I am trying to keep that light burning inside me, but it is very hard to keep a flame alive when you are standing in a wind that wants to blow it out. I just hope that by the time I have her in my arms, I can look back at this time and say I was stronger than I felt. I have to believe that. I have no other choice.
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

Oh, I turn to the voices in my memory. When the worry starts to climb into my throat, I don't go to the clinic portal first. I call my mother, or I close my eyes and try to remember exactly how my *abuela* would make the tea for when you are restless. I remember the songs they hummed when they were tired. That is my true health care. That is the only place where I feel safe and heard, even if it is only in my own mind.

If I cannot reach my mother, I go to the Internet, but I am very careful. I search for things in Spanish—*remedios naturales*, things that mothers have shared for a hundred years. I look for advice that feels kind, not clinical. Sometimes, I find these little forums for migrant mothers, and reading their words feels like a soft blanket. They don't talk about "BMI" or "percentiles." They talk about the *susto*, about the feeling of being far from home, about the way the baby kicks when you are lonely. 

It is strange, but I trust these women more than the doctors in their white coats. They understand that I am not just a body; they understand that I am a woman carrying a heart within my heart. The doctors are there for the anatomy, I suppose, but these women—they are the ones who are keeping my spirit alive. They are the ones who remind me that I am strong enough to be a mother, even when the world here tries to tell me I am not.

I think I have realized something, even while talking to you today. I spent so much of my pregnancy feeling like I had to be a "good patient"—quiet, obedient, ready with the right answers—but I am starting to see that this is a trap. Being a "good patient" to them just means being a small version of myself. 

When I look at my daughter, I don't want her to grow up feeling like she has to ask for permission to be herself. If I can be brave enough to ask more questions—or even to just say, "I am scared" or "I need you to explain this differently"—maybe that will be the first lesson I teach her. It is very hard, though. The air in the clinic is still so thin, and the words still feel like stones in my mouth. 

I just want to get to the day when the waiting is over. I imagine myself holding her, finally, and I think that maybe, in that moment, all the noise from the appointments will just fall away. She will look at me, and I will be her mother, and she won’t care about my weight or my English or the forms I signed. She will just know that I am the one who kept her safe. That is what I hold onto. The rest... the rest is just noise, isn't it? I just have to keep walking, one day at a time, until I am holding the only answer that matters.
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

It is almost always "informed." Rarely, if ever, is it "sought." 

When I walk into that exam room, the plan is already waiting for me on the desk. They have these forms—"The Birth Plan," they call it—but they aren't asking me what *my* dreams for the labor are. They are asking me to choose from a menu they already wrote. *Do you want this medication? Do you want this procedure?* They ask me these questions before they even know if I understand what the words mean in English. It feels like a test where I have to guess the right answer to be considered a "good mother."

There was one time, only one, where a nurse—not a doctor, just a nurse—actually sat down and asked me, "Clara, what are you most afraid of?" I didn't know what to say at first, because nobody had ever asked me about my feelings before. I told her about being alone, about not having my mother to hold my hand, and about my fear that I wouldn't know what to do when the baby cried. She didn't have a clipboard in her hand then. She just listened. And in that moment, I felt like she was actually looking at *me*. 

That was the only time I felt like my voice had any weight in that building. Every other time, it is just them telling me what will happen to my body, as if it is a car they are repairing. I am a passenger in my own pregnancy, watching the scenery go by, waiting for them to tell me which way we are turning next. I want to be the driver, but I don't know how to take the wheel when they act like I don't know the road.

I remember that day with the nurse so clearly. It was the first time I felt like a human being in that clinic, not just a set of test results. She didn’t look at the clock once. She didn’t talk about iron or weight gain. She just looked at my face, and for a moment, the walls of the clinic seemed to soften. I felt like I could finally breathe.

But the day after, I went back to see a different provider—a doctor I’d never met—and it was like being shoved back into a freezer. She didn't know about that conversation. She didn't care about my fears. She just flipped through the pages, asked me if I’d had my "routine screenings," and moved on. It was so jarring. I felt like I had to build that bridge of trust all over again, but the materials to build it—my vulnerability, my openness—had already been used up the day before. 

It makes me wonder if these clinics know how much power they hold over us. We are at our most fragile, carrying life, and yet we are treated like strangers who need to be processed as quickly as possible. I am not a project to be finished or a "case" to be solved. I am a mother. And sometimes, just being treated with a little bit of patience is the best medicine they could ever give me. I keep holding onto the hope that, when the labor finally starts, I will find the strength to demand that respect, not for myself, but for her—for my daughter—so she knows that her mother was the one who made the decisions, not the machines.
Was there a moment when your trust in a provider was broken — something they said, missed, or failed to explain?

There was a time, maybe a month ago, that I will never forget. I had been feeling so tired—not just normal, sleepy-tired, but a heavy, grey kind of exhaustion that made it hard to even walk from the bus stop to my apartment. I tried to tell the midwife about it. I searched for the right words in my head—I wanted to say that my blood felt thin, that I felt like I was disappearing.

She didn't look up from her screen. She just typed something, clicked a button, and said, "It is normal, Clara. Every woman feels this. Just take your vitamins and go home." She said it so fast, like she was finishing a sentence I hadn't even started. 

I felt a coldness go right through me. It wasn't just that she didn't hear the exhaustion; it was that she didn't care to ask *why* I was so tired. She didn't know I had been working extra shifts to pay for the baby’s clothes, or that I was eating very little so I could save for the hospital bills. When she dismissed me, it was like she was dismissing my entire life, my struggle, and the sacrifices I was making every single day. 

In that moment, my trust didn't just crack—it shattered. I realized then that if I were truly in danger, if something was really wrong with me or the baby, she wouldn't see it because she wasn't looking at me. She was looking at a box she had to check. I left that clinic feeling more alone than I did when I walked in, and I didn't go back for two weeks because I didn't want to face that indifference again. I had to learn, right then, that I am the only one who is really watching over me.

It is hard to keep going back to a place where you feel like you are just a mistake waiting to happen in their records. After that day, I started keeping a small notebook in my pocket. I write down what I want to ask—not just about my body, but about the *fears* I have. I practice the words in my head before I go in, trying to make my English sound smooth and sure, even when my heart is knocking against my ribs like a trapped bird.

But sometimes, when I get into that room, all the words just... *vanish*. I look at the provider—whoever it is today—and I see they are in a hurry, their hand already on the doorknob, and I think, *Why bother?* Why give them a piece of my truth if they are just going to put it in a chart and forget it? 

I think the biggest thing I have learned is that this "care" I was promised isn't really for me. It is for the hospital, for the system, for the laws. I am the one who has to be the doctor of my own soul. I have to be the one who tells myself, "You are doing okay, Clara. You are strong. You are going to be a good mother." If I wait for them to tell me that, I think I will be waiting for a very long time. I just pray that when I am in the labor room, and I am at my most vulnerable, I can find a way to be heard, not just processed. I have to be my daughter’s voice before she can even speak for herself.
Were there appointments, scans, or care moments where your partner's absence left you feeling isolated or unsupported?

It is... hard to describe the silence of that room when you are sitting there alone. There are times when I am waiting for the ultrasound, and I see the other women—the ones with their husbands, or their mothers, or their friends—and they are holding hands, looking at the screen together, laughing at the little movements. I look down at my own empty seat, and the loneliness is so heavy I can barely breathe.

My partner, he works so hard to save for the hospital, but he is never there for the *moments*. He is not there when the baby kicks for the first time on the monitor. He is not there when the midwife gives the news, or when the nurses ask about my health. When I come home, I try to tell him, "Today, she moved like this," or "Today, the doctor said she is healthy," but the words lose their magic when I have to repeat them. 

It makes me feel like I am building this child in a private world that no one else really knows. When I am sitting on that table, exposed and nervous, having someone to squeeze my hand would mean everything. It would mean that this isn't just *my* burden to bear. Without him there, I feel like I am just a visitor in the clinic, a stranger who is waiting for something that I have to face completely on my own. It makes me wonder—when she finally comes, will he know how to hold her? Or will he feel like a stranger to her, too?

Sometimes, I wonder if the clinic knows that my isolation isn't just about the physical room. It is about the gap between my life and theirs. When I walk out of that door, I don't go home to a nursery filled with soft blankets and lullabies that I learned here. I go home to my small apartment, to the bills, to the silence of my own thoughts, and to the weight of everything I am trying to build for my daughter.

I wish there was a moment where they would ask about that—not to be intrusive, but just to understand that my care is not just about the baby's heartbeat. It is about how I am going to survive the first months, how I am going to find the strength to breastfeed when I am so tired from working, how I am going to be the mother she deserves when I feel like I am just a student of this country. 

I don't need them to fix my life. I know they can't. But I need them to acknowledge that my life is the foundation for hers. If they could just see that, if they could just look at me and say, "Clara, this is hard, but you are not doing it alone," I think I would leave those appointments feeling like a mother, not just a statistic. I’m not asking for much—just a little kindness to fill the space where my mother’s voice should be.
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

I do it every night. My phone is my most trusted friend, even if it feels a little cold in my hands. I don't go there to look for symptoms—I leave that to the nurses and their clinical lists—I go there to find *answers to my heart*. 

I look for the things they never mention. I search for how to soothe a baby when you are alone in a city where you don't speak the language. I look for stories from other women who have had to cross a border, just so I can feel like I am not the only one. I look for simple, gentle advice on how to hold a newborn when you are terrified you will break them. I want to know about the *small* things—how to know if she is sleeping safely, or what to do when you have no family to pass you the baby so you can sleep. 

The clinic gives me papers about "warning signs" and "complications." They tell me how to spot a fever or when to call 911. But they never tell me how to find the courage to be a mother when your own mother is thousands of miles away. They never explain the feeling of the empty room, or how to handle the quiet when the baby finally goes to sleep and you are left with your own fears. I search for comfort, I think. I search for a way to bridge the gap between their cold, clinical instructions and the warm, messy, beautiful reality of becoming a mother. I am trying to build a mother's wisdom from pieces of glass I find on the internet, because the people who are supposed to be my experts only ever talk to me about my iron.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

You know, there is one thing. I want people to know that I am not just a story of "risk" or "migration." I am not just a set of blood tests that come back clear or cloudy. When I walk out of that clinic, I carry a whole world in my belly. I carry my mother’s prayers, my own dreams, and the hope that my daughter will never have to feel as small as I do when I’m sitting on that paper-covered table.

I wish that the healthcare system here—the one with all the bright lights and the fast computers—could learn that *trust* is the most important medicine. They give me lists and brochures, but they don't give me their time. And yet, time is the only thing that would make me feel like I am finally settling into this new land.

I just want to be seen. Not as a patient, not as a visitor, but as a woman who is trying to bridge two worlds. If they could just look at me—not at the chart, not at the screen, but at *me*—and say, "Clara, you are doing a brave thing," I think I could get through anything. I am terrified, yes, but I am also stronger than they could ever know. I just hope that by the time she is here, I will have found the voice to tell them that. Thank you for listening to me today. It is the first time I have felt like my words really meant something here.
